nodes.title: CL:Applied Animal Behavior
nodes.description: Uses a scientific approach to answer fundamental questions about animal behavior such as how and why animals behave a particular way; primarily taught from an applied perspective using knowledge to investigate and solve practical, real-world problems. Students will learn how to observe and record behavior and apply this knowledge to a semester-long project. Broad areas of behavior including communication, feeding and social behavior will also be explored. Prerequisite: ASCI 2130. Pre/Co-requisites: ASCI 1990 recommended when the topic is research skills.
nodes.other_requirements: instructor permission. ASCI 1990 Introduction to Research Skills is a suggested pre-requisite for this class. Students are responsible for their own transportation.

nodes.title: Undergraduate Research
nodes.description: Undergraduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor Permission required

nodes.title: Physiology of Reproduction
nodes.description: Fundamental principles of the physiology of reproduction with emphasis on, but not limited to, farm animals. Prerequisite: ASCI 2110, ASCI 2120; or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission

nodes.title: Zoos, Exotics & Endang Species
nodes.description: From gorillas to golden lion tamarinds, how human attitudes, activities, utilization, and management strategies impact wild and captive animal populations. Prerequisites: ASCI 1000; BIOL 1000, BIOL 1400, or BCOR 1400.
nodes.other_requirements: Prereqs enforced by the system

nodes.title: Winter Dairy Travel Course
nodes.description: An extended field trip to a variety of dairy farms and dairy related businesses in the Northeast with students and faculty from other New England land grant institutions including UCONN, UNH, UMASS Amherst, URI, and UMaine. Offered over winter break, a different area is visited annually. Prerequisite: ASCI 1000.
nodes.other_requirements: PACE students by permission and override.

nodes.title: Equine Practicum
nodes.description: Geared towards students with little to no horse experience, focuses on hands-on skills at both the UVM Horse Barn and the UVM Morgan Horse Farm. Students will meet weekly at the UVM Horse Barn to gain experience with haltering, grooming, leading, lunging, and assessing horses. Includes learning how to identify horses, disciplines, and physical condition. Prerequisite: ASCI 1000 or ASCI 1400.
nodes.other_requirements: Open to degree and PACE students. Some Saturday meetings TBD. Students will be responsible for their own transportation to and from the farm.

nodes.title: Undergraduate Research
nodes.description: Undergraduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion. Prerequisites: Junior standing; Department Chair permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Junior standing; Department Chair permission. Instructor Permission required

nodes.title: Clin Topics:Livestock Medicine
nodes.description: An advanced study of diseases in cattle, sheep, goats, and pigs, emphasizing disease detection, pathobiology, treatment and prevention. Prerequisites: ASCI 2180, ASCI 2110, ASCI 2120.
nodes.other_requirements: Junior standing and Instructor Permission Required

nodes.title: One Health: an Exploration
nodes.description: Explores the interconnection of human, animal, and environmental health, covering topics like pollution, zoonoses, and comparative medicine. Examines the science behind these issues and what can be done about them and the role of other factors such as economics, culture, and the skills needed to tackle them.

nodes.title: Intro to Animal Nutrition
nodes.description: Comprehensive study of specific nutrients in terms of their digestion, availability, function, and utilization in animals.

nodes.title: Horse Barn Cooperative
nodes.description: Develops skills in the practical aspects of equine management of individual horses and horses maintained in a group setting using hands-on experiences and peer teaching. Students care for their own horse or an Animal Science horse. Prerequisites: For students currently accepted into the UVM Horse Barn Cooperative Program or currently enrolled in ASCI 2400; Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission

nodes.title: Equus
nodes.description: A hands-on equine management experience. Students perform horse duties, recordkeeping, and make financial and management decisions on a horse boarding operation. Prerequisites: ASCI 1000 or ASCI 1400.
nodes.other_requirements: instructor permission; 2-4 variable credits; Minimum sophomore standing

nodes.title: Horse Health and Disease
nodes.description: After an introduction to equine anatomy and physiology, students are presented with common diseases and their corresponding description, cause, clinical signs, diagnosis, treatment, prognosis and prevention. Weekly small-group case studies highlight core principles. Optional hands-on opportunities at UVM Horse Barn. Prerequisite: ASCI 1000 or ASCI 1400.
nodes.other_requirements: Prereqs enforced by the system: Open to degree and PACE students

nodes.title: Advanced Animal Nutrition
nodes.description: Discusses the principles of meeting the nutrient requirements of animals, including an introduction to feedstuffs, animal metabolism and feed formulation for domestic or captive vertebrate animals. Prerequisites: ASCI 1040, ASCI 2110; or Instructor permission. Pre/co-requisite: ASCI 2120.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission. Must register for a lab L01-L04. Open to Degree and PACE students.

nodes.title: Independent Study
nodes.description: A course which is tailored to fit the interests of a specific student, which occurs outside the traditional classroom/laboratory setting under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required. PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: France: The European Pony Tour
nodes.description: See Schedule of Courses for specific titles.
nodes.other_requirements: Course meets off campus at the Humane Society of Chittenden County. This class will meet weekly at the Humane Society of Chittenden County at 142 Kindness Court, South Burlington, VT, 05403 (about 3 miles from campus). This is a civic learning course, meaning that students are learning while volunteering to help their community. Students are expected to be at the shelter on time and in appropriate clothing. Depending on enrollment numbers, students may be assigned a shift within the window of time that the course meets (likely 12:00-1:30 or 1:30-3:00, though you may not be able to select your shift) to be at the shelter. During their time at the shelter, students can expect to work hard - work which may involve getting dirty. The ability to work in contact with animals and follow directions are essential (direct contact with animals is likely but not guaranteed). While the shelter we will be at will only euthanize animals if it is in the best interest of the animal or for the safety of the community, students should realize that they may interact with animals that could potentially have that outcome. This course is suitable for students with no experience in an animal shelter, as it will focus on the basics of what it takes to run an animal shelter and the role of animal shelters within the community. Students with experience in animal rescue are welcome, but must realize that this course is geared towards an introductory level. This course does not take place in a typical classroom environment; students will take on the role of volunteer in an animal shelter during this course. The exact tasks students will complete will vary from week to week, but cleaning, enrichment preparation, managerial duties, and other types of animal care may be expected. Students will also likely be assigned mandatory supplemental resources (such as videos, articles, and podcasts) to complete outside of class time and expected to be able to discuss this content during class. Students can expect to spend up to six hours outside of class time on this class, per University policy regarding work required for a three credit hour course.

nodes.title: Equine Mgmt and Programming
nodes.description: Designed for the leadership groups in the Hardacre Equine Center. Students who are on the Horse Barn Executive Committee, Equus Advisors, and Dressage Team Captains are required to participate. Topics include equine nutrition, farm management, equine health and wellness, pasture management, equine equipment management and selection, and biosecurity protocols. Depending on the student's role there are additional horse care, teaching, and student management responsibilities. Prerequisite: Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission. Students who are on the Horse Barn Executive Committee, Equus Advisors, and Dressage Team Captains are required to participate.

nodes.title: Independent Study
nodes.description: A course which is tailored to fit the interests of a specific student, which occurs outside the traditional classroom/laboratory setting under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor Permission required

nodes.title: Accelerated Biology
nodes.description: Selected topics from the full year of introductory biology, compressed into one semester. For students with demonstrated mastery of basic biology (e.g., AP credit). Permission required. Credit not awarded for both BCOR 1425 and BCOR 1400 or BIOL 1400. Pre/co-requisite: CHEM 1400, CHEM 1405, or CHEM 1410.
nodes.other_requirements: For students with demonstrated mastery of basic biology (e.g., AP credit). Permission required. Credit not awarded for both BCOR 1425 and BCOR 1400 or BIOL 1400. By invitation only; for FTFY students with AP score of 4. Instructor permission required. Must register for a BCOR 1425 lab. Must be enrolled in CHEM 1400 if have no CHEM credit.

nodes.title: Equine Industry Issues
nodes.description: Case-based course enhances students' abilities to integrate information, use logical thought processes, and produce concise, organized solutions to real problems, from individual horses to industry-wide. Prerequisites: ASCI 2470, ASCI 2400, ASCI 2130, or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission

nodes.title: Clin Topics Equine Med & Surg
nodes.description: Students work through medical and surgical cases from chief complaint to treatment, prognosis and prevention. Diagnostic techniques and treatment options prioritized. Hands-on opportunities include physical, orthopedic, and neurologic exams, as well as field trips to local equine facilities and the UVM Morgan Horse Farm. Prerequisite: ASCI 2480.

nodes.title: Outline: General Chem w/lab
nodes.description: One-semester survey of principles and concepts of general chemistry, topics covered include bonding, mole ratios, equilibrium, and nuclear chemistry. Credit not awarded for both CHEM 1100 and CHEM 1102, CHEM 1400, or CHEM 1405.
nodes.other_requirements: Credit not awarded for both CHEM 1100 and CHEM 1102, CHEM 1400, or CHEM 1405. $60 fee for lab supplies; Colocated with CHEM 1102A; Must register for CHEM 1100 lab L01-L07; Non-CAS, non - BSAD students only; Open to degree and PACE students.

nodes.title: Animals in Soc/Animal Welfare
nodes.description: Designed to heighten awareness and understanding of human-animal relationships in society, agriculture, and science. Pre/Co-requisites: ASCI 1000, ASCI 1400, or ASCI 1500.
nodes.other_requirements: Sophomore standing or above; or instructor permission, ASCI majors and minors

nodes.title: Intro to the Horse
nodes.description: Starting with evolution and domestication and progressing to current breeds, colors, uses, health, and management of horses, provides a basic understanding of one of our most beloved domestic animals. No prior horse experience or knowledge is required.

nodes.title: Equine Enterprise Management
nodes.description: Provides guidelines for understanding risks, liabilities and other pertinent topics necessary for running a successful equine-related business. Prerequisite: ASCI 1000 or ASCI 1400.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission

nodes.title: Appl Animal Health
nodes.description: A study of small and large domestic animal diseases. Natural response to disease, methods of diagnosis, control, and treatment. Prerequisite: ASCI 1000, a Biology course, or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission.

nodes.title: Understanding & Speaking Dog
nodes.description: With dogs as a model, explores the impact of genetic modification and selection, neonatal to adult development of the brain, the science of how the brain learns, human involvement and its impact, and the factual language of dogs. Prerequisite: Animal Science major or minor, Psychological Science major.
nodes.other_requirements: Animal Science major or minor, Psychological Science major. The ability to be near live demo dogs is required. Open to Degree and PACE students

nodes.title: Int:Morgan HorseFarmInternship
nodes.description: On-site supervised work experience combined with a structured academic learning plan directed by a faculty member or a faculty-staff team in which a faculty member is the instructor of record, for which academic credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion. Prerequisite: Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission. Instructor permission Required. Learning contact must be in before add drop. Please plan ahead! Please contact ASCI.INT@uvm.edu for overrides

nodes.title: Introductory Animal Sciences
nodes.description: An overview of the genetics, nutrition, reproduction, and management of livestock and recreation species; introduction to animal behavior, animal disease, and biotechnology. Prerequisite: Animal Science major, minor, or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Animal Science major, minor, or Instructor permission. Must register for an ASCI 1000 lab; ASCI first-year students only; Materials fee: $51; One mandatory Wed seminar

nodes.title: The World of Working Animals
nodes.description: Explores the diversity of working animals including many lesser known animals such as landmine-sniffing Gambian pouched rats and military marine mammals, the people with which they work, the context of their job, the human-animal bonds that are formed, and some of the challenges faced.

nodes.title: CREAM 1
nodes.description: The first of a two-course sequence. Students perform the work and make the financial and management decisions associated with the CREAM dairy herd. Prerequisites: ASCI 1000 or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required; PACE students by permission and override.

nodes.title: Advanced Zoo Topics
nodes.description: An exploration of modern zoo philosophy and ethics and the extent of human intervention necessary for the preservation of endangered species. Prerequisites: ASCI 2600 or Instructor permission.

nodes.title: Endocrinology
nodes.description: Physiology of endocrine and autocrine/paracrine systems and growth factors. Prerequisites: ASCI 2120; BIOL 1000 and BIOL 1005, BIOL 1000 and BCOR 1425, BIOL 1400 and BIOL 1450, or BCOR 1400 and BCOR 1450.
nodes.other_requirements: Prereqs enforced by the system. Open to degree and PACE students. This upper level course is for students with good background in biology, physiology, and anatomy.

nodes.title: Honors
nodes.description: Honors course.

nodes.title: General Chemistry 1
nodes.description: First semester of a two-semester sequence. Topics include matter, stoichiometry, gas laws, thermochemistry, quantum theory, atomic structure, electronic configurations, bonding, and intermolecular forces. Credit not awarded for both CHEM 1400 and CHEM 1100, CHEM 1102, or CHEM 1405.

nodes.title: Animal Genetics
nodes.description: The study of DNA with an emphasis in genetics of animal species, included but not limited to livestock and companion animals. Topics include patterns of inheritance, molecular genetics, gene regulation, biotechnology, genomics, population and quantitative genetics. Prerequisite: BIOL 1400, BIOL 1450, BCOR 1400, BCOR 1450, or BCOR 1425.
nodes.other_requirements: Prereqs enforced by the system:BIOL 1400orBIOL 1450orBCOR 1450orBCOR 1425orBCOR 1400; Open to degree and PACE students

nodes.title: Animal Anatomy
nodes.description: A comprehensive study of anatomical structure of vertebrate animals with emphasis on domestic animals. Taught from a systemic anatomy approach and incorporating microscopic and developmental anatomy, comparative vertebrate anatomy, and applied/clinical anatomy. Some physiology will be introduced to reinforce the link between structure and function. Prerequisites: BIOL 1000, BIOL 1400, or BCOR 1400; CHEM 1100 or CHEM 1400.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for a lab: A01 - A05; or Instructor permission.

nodes.title: Independent Study
nodes.description: A course which is tailored to fit the interests of a specific student, which occurs outside the traditional classroom/laboratory setting under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor Permission required. Offered at department discretion.

nodes.title: Internship
nodes.description: On-site supervised work experience combined with a structured academic learning plan directed by a faculty member or a faculty-staff team in which a faculty member is the instructor of record, for which academic credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor Permission required. Requires a faculty mentor and chair approval. Open to degree and PACE students.

nodes.title: Bovine Reproduction Workshop
nodes.description: Laboratory for fundamental principles of the physiology of reproduction with emphasis on, but not limited to, farm animals. Must be taken concurrently with ASCI 3150. Topics vary by offering; periodic offering at intervals that may exceed four years. Prerequisites: ASCI 2110, ASCI 2120; or Instructor permission. Co-requisite: ASCI 3150.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission. Co-requisite: ASCI 3150. Instructor Permission Required. Pre- and Post-Online work, (spring break dates TBD- 2 days), 8 am 3 pm both days; for the course such as readings, info session, and a debriefing will occur. Transportation provided from Miller farm leaving at 8 am to Kayhart dairy in W Addison VT returning no later than 3pm both days. Students must provide their own coveralls and boots but learning materials will be provided. Course Fee: $165. Open to Degree and PACE students. This is a 2 day course over spring break. Students must attend two consecutive days the week of spring break (exact dates TBD). This course occurs at an off campus farm (transportation provided) both days from ~8-4 pm. Students will meet at the Miller Farm to then head off campus. Attend 2 day off campus workshop over spring break to complete Artificial Insemination training through GENEX.

nodes.title: Intro to Dairy Management
nodes.description: See Schedule of Courses for specific titles.

nodes.title: Teaching Assistantship
nodes.description: Undergraduate student service as a teaching assistant, usually in an introductory-level course in the discipline, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor Permission required; PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: CREAM 2
nodes.description: The second of a two-course sequence. Students perform the work and make the financial and management decisions associated with the CREAM dairy herd. Prerequisites: ASCI 2300.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor Permission required

nodes.title: Teaching Assistantship
nodes.description: Undergraduate student service as a teaching assistant, usually in an introductory level course in the discipline, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor Permission required; Offered for 1-3 variable PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: Principles of Biology 2
nodes.description: Principles of organismal biology; nature of scientific inquiry, plant form and function, pollination ecology, animal phylogeny illustrated by comparative anatomy and physiology; animal behavior. Credit not awarded for both BIOL 1450 and BCOR 1450.
nodes.other_requirements: ASCI, BIOL, BISC, MGEN, MICR, NEUR, PBIO, ZOOL majors should register for BCOR 1450; Degree students only even after level restrictions removed; Post Bac Pre med students register for BIOL 1450

nodes.title: Intro to the Cat and Dog
nodes.description: This is a whirlwind tour of the science of our best friends - the cat and dog! Students will learn about different aspects of our most popular companion species, such as their history, biology, behavior, and health.

nodes.title: Advanced Dairy Management
nodes.description: An intensive, residential program at the Miner Institute providing an in-depth experiential program in the management of the dairy herd. Prerequisite: Junior standing or Farms 2+2 enrollment.
nodes.other_requirements: Meets at WH Miner Agricultural Institute in Chazy, NY;

nodes.title: Animal Career Seminar
nodes.description: Discussion and workshop activities exploring careers in animal and food science. Includes resume preparation and interview training. Prerequisite: ASCI 1000.

nodes.title: Ugr Hum Anatomy & Physiology 1
nodes.description: Part I of two-semester course sequence. Structure and function of human body.
nodes.other_requirements: Prereg limited to: BMCS, BIOM, CSD, EMS, EXSC, HSCI, MLS, MRS, NFS, NMT, PE, PHS, PRNU majors only. Degree students only even after major restrictions lifted.

nodes.title: General Physiology
nodes.description: A comprehensive review of the physiology of mammalian animals. Prerequisites: ASCI 2110, or ANPS 1190 and ANPS 1200; BIOL 1000, BIOL 1400, or BCOR 1400.
nodes.other_requirements: iClicker is required for all students to be used for in class practice. Students are expected to read the assigned materials of anatomy of each system prior to class time and take a prelecture quiz using Blackboard (counts 5% of your final points). You should also read it thoroughly after the lecture and outline the material from the book. Taking exams provides a valuable opportunity to review material you have learned in class and to examine the depth with which you understand concepts. Studying for exams can involve a lot of memorization. Take home assignments are the “test yourself” questions in your text book covered in each lecture. You do not need to turn the assignments in, but regularly, some exam questions will be directly taken from those questions. No cell phone should be used during class and exams. Personal computer may be used during lectures at the last rows of classroom. For students who take exams at the Exam Proctoring Center, please book your times overlapped with the class exam times unless they are not possible with acceptable reasons.

nodes.title: Principles of Biology 1
nodes.description: Principles of cellular biochemistry; cell biology; genetics and evolution. Topics: biochemistry; metabolism, cell structure/function; respiration; photosynthesis; molecular, Mendelian and population genetics; genetics of evolution. Credit not awarded for both BIOL 1400 and BCOR 1400 or BCOR 1425.
nodes.other_requirements: Credit not awarded for both BIOL 1400 and BCOR 1400 or BCOR 1425. Must register for a BIOL 1400 lab; Lab supply fee: $30; Degree students only even after level restrictions removed; Post Bac Pre Med & PACE students register for BIOL 1400 ZRA Colocated with BIOL 1400 ZRA

nodes.title: Lameness in Horses
nodes.description: Focuses on normal equine anatomy related to movement and what happens when horses are injured. Students learn common causes of lameness, as well as how to diagnose, treat, and prevent those causes. Labs are hands-on with horses. Prerequisites: ASCI 2480.
nodes.other_requirements: Online work before Lec/Lab is required. Open to Degree and PACE Students

nodes.title: CREAM Advising
nodes.description: Augments learning acquired during previous CREAM experience; students provide technical, logistical, organizational support to the current group of CREAM students. Prerequisite: ASCI 2300 or ASCI 2310.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor Permission required; Open to degree and PACE students

nodes.title: Internship
nodes.description: On-site supervised work experience combined with a structured academic learning plan directed by a faculty member or a faculty-staff team in which a faculty member is the instructor of record, for which academic credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required. Learning contract must be done before add drop. Please plan ahead! Please contact ASCI.INT@uvm.edu for overrides. PACE students by permission and override

nodes.title: Exploring Biology 1
nodes.description: Exploring biology from cells to organisms. Topics include origins of life, ancestral organisms, uni- and multi- cellular energetics, evolution of respiration and metabolism, and the genetic code. Credit not awarded for both BCOR 1400 and BIOL 1400 or BCOR 1425.

nodes.title: Exploring Biology 2
nodes.description: An evolutionary perspective to exploring biology. Topics include: patterns of inheritance, Darwinian evolution, evolution of biodiversity, ecology of organisms, human effects on biological systems. Credit not awarded for both BCOR 1450 and BIOL 1450.

nodes.title: Ugr Hum Anatomy & Physiology 2
nodes.description: Part II of two-semester course sequence. Structure and function of human body. Prerequisite: ANPS 1190.
nodes.other_requirements: Common hour exams are held 5:30-7:00pm on these Mondays: 2/9, 3/2, and 4/6. BIOM,CSD,DIET,DNFS,EXSC,HSCI,MLS,NFS, PE, PHS, MRS, PRNU; Majors only until Dec. 2nd, Please contact Patrick Mullen (patrick.c.mullen@uvm.edu) with any questions; Class meets 3x a week with online lab comprising 4th credit; Degree students only even after level restrictions removed; PACE students register for ANPS 1200 ZRA; Prereq enforced by the system:ANPS 1190.
